title: North Indian Samosa

subs.name: whole wheat flour (atta) gives a nuttier, denser pastry; knead with a slightly higher oil ratio to compensate
finely diced boiled carrots and beans make a colourful mixed-vegetable filling
a squeeze of half a lemon added to the filling just before it comes off the heat

steps.text: Make the pastry dough: combine maida, ajwain, a generous pinch of salt, and 2 tablespoons of oil in a bowl. Rub the oil into the flour with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs — this fat coating is what gives samosa its characteristic flaky bite. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, mixing until a firm but pliable dough forms. It should be stiffer than roti dough. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 20 minutes.

While the dough rests, prepare the filling. Heat 1 tablespoon of oil in a kadai or frying pan over medium heat. Add cumin seeds and wait for them to splutter. Add green chilli and grated ginger and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the peas and cook for 2 minutes. Tip in the boiled potato and mix well, breaking any large lumps. Add ground coriander, amchur, red chilli powder, garam masala, and salt. Stir and cook for 2–3 minutes until everything is well combined and the mixture is dry, not wet. Add coriander leaves if using. Cool the filling before stuffing.

Divide the rested dough into 8 equal balls. Roll each ball into an oval about 16 cm long and 8 cm wide, slightly thicker in the centre. Cut each oval in half to get two semicircles. Fold one semicircle into a cone shape, sealing the straight edge with a dab of water and pressing firmly. Hold the cone in your palm, fill two-thirds with cooled potato filling, then fold the open top over to form a triangular flap and press all seams shut tightly — any gaps will cause the samosa to open in the oil.

Pour oil into a deep kadai to a depth of at least 8 cm and heat over medium flame to around 160°C. Test by dropping a small piece of dough — it should rise slowly without browning instantly. Slide in 4–5 samosas at a time without crowding. Fry slowly, turning occasionally, for 12–15 minutes until the shells are an even golden-amber colour and feel firm and hollow when tapped. Drain on paper towels. Do not fry over high heat or the pastry will colour too fast while the inside stays raw and doughy.

Serve hot with tamarind chutney and mint chutney on the side. Samosas are best eaten within 30 minutes of frying while the shell is at its crispest. Leftover samosas can be re-crisped in an air fryer at 180°C for 5 minutes.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: maida → whole wheat flour (atta) gives a nuttier, denser pastry; knead with a slightly higher oil ratio to compensate; peas → finely diced boiled carrots and beans make a colourful mixed-vegetable filling; amchur → a squeeze of half a lemon added to the filling just before it comes off the heat
